title: How to apply Ngā Tikanga Paihere to microdata research projects | Stats NZ
content:
## How to apply Ngā Tikanga Paihere to microdata research projects
This information about Ngā Tikanga Paihere will help researchers identify potential impacts of their research and show evidence of their responsiveness to Treaty of Waitangi and human rights obligations in their application for access to microdata in Stats NZ’s Data Lab.

## About Ngā Tikanga Paihere (The Tikanga Framework)
In 2018, Stats NZ partnered with Māui Hudson, Associate Professor at Te Pua Wānanga ki te Ao, Faculty of Māori and Indigenous Studies, University of Waikato to develop Ngā Tikanga Paihere. This is a framework designed to help researchers engage and partner with iwi and Māori to ensure the use of microdata is respectful, ethical, and culturally appropriate. The first iteration of this framework was released on 31 March 2020, but it will evolve to better reflect progress in the Māori Data Governance space. The underlying principles of Ngā Tikanga Paihere applies also to other marginalised community.
Ngā Tikanga Paihere has more detail about the framework and its principles.

## Identifying suitable relationships
Suitable relationships are partnership-relationships that researchers have established with whānau, hapū, iwi, Māori advisory groups, special councils, community members, expert advisors, or interested groups who are assisting the research.
The following are examples from previous Data Lab applications:
- Health researchers consulted Ngāi Tahu Research Consultation Committee for Māori consultation and a Māori health expert. They plan to have an ongoing consultation with Māori researchers.
- Māori researchers actively engage with iwi groups and key stakeholders of Te Puni Kōkiri, the Ministry of Māori Development.
- Researchers experienced with interpreting sensitive data consult with Mātauranga Māori researchers. They plan to engage with relevant government agencies should risks arise.
- Researchers investigating a topic relating to disability work closely with disability groups and consult with a senior disability educator. To facilitate this, they plan to establish a Disability Data Reference Group.

## Balancing relationships and bias in your research
Leveraging your existing relationships should be done in a way that adds value to your research, and helps you to:
- seek insights and perspectives to help make sense of your research findings
- interpret your research in an ethical or culturally sensitive way
- identify anything that you might have overlooked or ignored in your analyses
- gain feedback about your research design and methodology.
We encourage researchers to engage with stakeholders who can make a meaningful contribution to research. We recognise not all stakeholders in the community of interest are suitable to engage with.
We trust you will examine your existing relationships and decide which would be most suited to your research purposes.

## Finding the right people to consult with
The right people have authority, expertise, or lived experience that makes them suitable to represent the sentiment of their communities. This could be whānau, hapū, iwi, Māori advisory groups, special councils, community members, expert advisors, or interested groups who can provide assistance with the research.

## Building relationships can take time – plan for it as part of your deadlines
If you don’t already have appropriate relationships as part of your research, it is crucial that you start building them, in time to meet your deadlines. This is because we assess the levels of support you have to ensure you are conducting your research or interpreting your findings in a culturally appropriate and ethical way.
We understand not all researchers have existing relationships they can leverage for research. We strongly recommend asking your institution or organisation if they have relevant external relationships with key partners and stakeholders. You may find suitable relationships for your project.
If this is not possible, we recommend you start building relationships with relevant stakeholders, including communities of interest. This can begin with an email exchange, a visit to a cultural advisor’s office, or conversations with colleagues.
Te Arawhiti – The Office for Māori Crown Relations offers guidance on how to create an engagement plan.
- Crown engagement with Māori
- Tools and resources .

## How to determine what is in the public interest
A project is in the public interest if it benefits New Zealand society as a whole or specific New Zealand communities. Research and analytic purposes that are in the public interest include purposes related to the functions and duties of government agencies such as developing and evaluating policy. It also includes a range of other research and analytic purposes where there is public good or benefit.
For example, we may consider whether projects are likely to provide value to:
- the goals of the communities of interest (including iwi and Māori) and those who will be impacted on by the data
- long-term wellbeing and development of New Zealanders
- stronger Māori-Crown relationships that might enhance iwi, hapū, and whānau development
- government priorities
- government inquiries into matters of public interest
- any community-led wellbeing initiatives that others could support and learn from.

## Showing evidence of accountability
We ask that you outline the measures that you have in place to ensure accountability to the communities of interest throughout your research project.
Accountability means you can show evidence that you are accountable to the communities impacted by your research findings. This includes any example or evidence demonstrating that the communities of interest, or their representatives, advisors, leaders, or advocates:
- understand the objectives of your research
- support your research, recognising its potential value to them or New Zealanders in general
- will be meaningfully engaged with as part of the research process for any reason, such as for insights from real-life situations, community perspectives, or help in making sense of research findings.

## Importance of involving community early with your project decisions
We want to see early involvement by communities of interest (or their representatives, advisors, leaders, or advocates) in your research decisions. We understand that there may be barriers to communicating your research to these communities if they lack technical knowledge, but we encourage researchers to work in collaboration with them.
Being involved in project decisions does not mean that communities of interest will be policing your project or dictating your work. Some forms of involvement could be:
- providing insights from real-life situations and perspectives to help make sense of your research findings
- guiding you in interpreting your research findings in an ethical or culturally sensitive way
- helping identify anything that you might have overlooked or ignored in your analyses
- advising about your research design and methodology.
Data in the Integrated Data Infrastructure or Longitudinal Business Database is real data from people and businesses in New Zealand. As data users, it is important you meaningfully engage with the communities the data is about.
Your research contributes towards a knowledge repository, and in some cases policy-related work, so it is important to be aware that communities could be impacted on by your research findings. By involving the communities of interest, you will be able to ensure your research provides benefits for them or other New Zealanders.

## How Data Lab applications are assessed
All Data Lab applications are reviewed in accordance with the Five Safes framework and Ngā Tikanga Paihere . These provide guidance for the subject matter, legal, and methodology teams responsible for the review process.
At the start of each cycle, the teams meet to discuss each application, allowing them to:
- share feedback about the applications
- raise and discuss any concerns identified in the applications
- ask questions and challenge each other
- plan the next steps.
If there are concerns to be addressed, or further clarification is required, these will be communicated to you. Once all concerns or questions have been addressed by you, your responses will feed into the final written review of the application that culminates in advice and a recommendation to the approver.
The approval is given by the Government Statistician or appropriate delegated person. Decisions for approval always involve some subjectivity as they are based on the advice and recommendations of the subject matter, legal, and methodology teams guided by the Five Safes framework and Ngā Tikanga Paihere . However, collaboration minimises any potential biases in the review and decision-making process.
Page updated 18 November 2020.
